text: In the operating room, the surgical team uses a twelve millimeter camera port in the left mid abdomen and two eight millimeter robotic ports on either side, along with a five millimeter assistant port near Palmer's point, to triangulate on the abscess cavity. Methylene blue is introduced into the bladder to help identify any urinary tract infection or bladder injury. The surgeon carefully dissects the ureacal cyst away from the anterior abdominal wall, removing the peritoneum and fatty tissues surrounding the median umbilical ligament without violating the fascia.

During the dissection, calcified tissue is encountered and set aside for removal later. The surgeon continues dissection along the peritoneum between the anterior abdominal wall and the median umbilical ligament, identifying a soft plane between the urachal cyst and the bladder. Careful dissection frees the urachal cyst from the bladder, achieving hemostasis and avoiding bladder injury.

The surgeon isolates the urachal cyst from the bladder, using bipolar cautery and scissors to ligate it. The median umbilical ligament is dissected free above the bladder level and similarly ligated with bipolar cautery and scissors.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the urachal cyst by removing the remaining tissue connecting it to the abdominal wall. The absence of methylene blue indicates no bladder entry, and the peritoneum is closed with a single layer of interrupted vicryl sutures.
